message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: METHODOLOGY §7 and GOVERNANCE §2 body text claim pre-registration is "RFC-3161 timestamped"; the engine ships only an HMAC authority (RFC-3161 is pluggable, not shipped/default)
DIMENSION: docs vs code drift / over-claims
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: docs/METHODOLOGY.md:130-132; docs/GOVERNANCE.md:55-62 METHODOLOGY.md:131
DESCRIPTION: Normative body text presents RFC-3161 timestamping as the mechanism actually in force. METHODOLOGY §7 L130-132: "Pre-registration: hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation." GOVERNANCE §2 L55-62 step 3: hypotheses are "RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority." But the engine ships exactly one timestamp authority — LocalHmacAuthority (on-box HMAC) — and no RFC-3161 TSA client (grep of src/ finds RFC-3161 only in comments/docstrings as a pluggable option; preregistration.py:130 LocalHmacAuthority is the only concrete authority). The runnable example (examples/minimal_study.py:38) and all defaults use LocalHmacAuthority. GOVERNANCE §2's own implementation-status note (L64-81) and ADR-0009 correctly hedge this ("ships one real, data-sovereign authority LocalHmacAuthority... and the Protocol so a study can plug an RFC-3161 TSA"), but the §7/§2 body claims read as if RFC-3161 (third-party non-repudiation) is what happens. HMAC is local tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret — explicitly NOT the third-party time attestation RFC-3161 provides. The mismatch matters because RFC-3161 is exactly the non-repudiation property a hostile-reviewer audience would key on.
SUGGESTED FIX: In the METHODOLOGY §7 bullet and GOVERNANCE §2 step 3, change the flat "RFC-3161 timestamped" to the shipped default with the pluggable option, e.g. "locked and cryptographically timestamped before confirmation (on-box HMAC by default — local tamper-evidence; an RFC-3161 TSA is pluggable for third-party non-repudiation)." Keep the body text consistent with the existing honest-scope notes.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual code and documentation.

content.content:
120	> (measurement → interpretation) and records the source measurement's ref. A study applies these
121	> types where judgment enters: LLM output from the reasoning seam is *raw judgment* and must be
122	> wrapped as an `Interpretation` (`judged_by` = the model id), never passed where a `Measurement`
123	> is expected. The engine enforces the direction structurally at the type boundary; it does not,
124	> and cannot, retro-classify arbitrary study values it never sees.
125	
126	## 7. Reproducibility
127	
128	- **Determinism:** fixed, documented seeds; inputs hashed; model names + versions recorded.
129	- **Provenance:** an append-only audit trail records every action before the next executes.
130	- **Pre-registration:** hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and
131	  RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation. This is honestly described as an adaptive
132	  design with a timestamped lock — not as predictions guessed before any data.
133	- **Reproducibility package:** data, code, configuration, and logs are published with
134	  findings so any reviewer can reconstruct the full decision history.
135	
136	## 8. Scoped-independence caveat
137	
138	When the baseline is built from text or artifacts that an external source also authored,
139	the baseline is independent of that source's **judgments**, but not of its **wording or
140	framing**. This is an acceptable, necessary compromise — but it is claimed as *scoped*
141	independence, stated explicitly, and is one of the reasons `indeterminate` exists.
142

45	  `run_id` as `thread_id`).
46	
47	**State-shape requirement for cloners:** gate decisions are returned as
48	`{"gate_decisions": [record]}` partial updates, so a study's graph state must declare
49	`gate_decisions` with a list/append reducer — otherwise each gate would overwrite the prior
50	one and the append-only trail would lose all but the last decision.
51	
52	## 2. Pre-registration
53	
54	Before any confirmatory test runs, the relevant hypotheses are:
55	
56	1. made specific and typed (what is claimed, the test, the data, the decision rule),
57	2. content-hashed and committed,
58	3. **RFC-3161 timestamped** against a trusted timestamp authority.
59	
60	This is honestly characterized as a **timestamped adaptive design**: methods are fixed
61	first; the data informs the questions; the questions are then locked and timestamped before
62	they are tested. It is not claimed to be "predictions registered before any data."
63	
64	> **Implementation status (engine):** pre-registration is enforced by construction **in the
65	> confirmatory runners** (`adjudicate`, `discover_and_confirm`; ADR-0009). They verify, via a
66	> supplied `TimestampAuthority`, that a hypothesis's proof **binds its content and id** (the
67	> canonical digest of its decision-bearing fields), that the attested **timestamp is
68	> verifiable**, and that the lock **precedes confirmation** — a hand-set sentinel string, a
69	> post-lock content/id swap, a forged proof, or a lock dated at/after the ordering instant are
70	> all refused. (In adjudication the ordering instant is captured *before the baseline build*, so
71	> a claim-derived hypothesis must be locked before the baseline exists; in discovery a
72	> data-surfaced hypothesis is necessarily locked inside the runner, so there the *content*
73	> binding is the load-bearing guarantee — see ADR-0009.) The engine ships one real,
74	> data-sovereign authority (`LocalHmacAuthority`, on-box HMAC) and the Protocol so a study can
75	> plug an RFC-3161 TSA. Honest scope: the local authority is tamper-evidence *relative to an
76	> on-box secret*, not third-party non-repudiation. The lower-level confirm *primitives*
77	> (`confirm_whole_corpus`/`confirm_unit_level`) gate on the cheap presence predicate by default
78	> and take an optional `authority=` to opt into the full check; a study that confirms outside a
79	> runner must pass it (or call `require_preregistered` itself). `Hypothesis.locked` is only a
80	> presence predicate; `methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered` is the
81	> methodology-grade check.
82	
83	## 3. Provenance
84	
85	An **append-only** audit trail (W3C PROV-DM style) records every action before the next
86	executes. No step can be retroactively removed or reordered. This is the spine of
87	reproducibility and of the independence claim.
88	
89	> **Implementation status (engine):** wired (ADR-0010). `assay_engine.provenance.ProvenanceTrail`

content.text:
Confirmed. The only concrete authority class is `LocalHmacAuthority`. `TimestampAuthority` and `StampingAuthority` are Protocols; no concrete RFC-3161 TSA client ships.

Now let me assess the key question — is the body text actually misleading, given the prominent implementation-status note immediately below it in GOVERNANCE, and the qualifier in METHODOLOGY §7. Let me re-read the METHODOLOGY bullet carefully and check the doc's own scope conventions.

input.description: All RFC-3161/HMAC mentions in both docs

content.text:
I have enough to render a verdict. Let me assess the two body-text claims precisely.

**METHODOLOGY §7 L130-132:** "hypotheses ... are locked and RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation." This flatly states RFC-3161 as the mechanism in force. There is NO inline qualifier; the follow-on sentence only addresses the adaptive-design honesty (predictions-before-data), not the timestamp mechanism. There is no implementation-status note in METHODOLOGY at all near this line.

**GOVERNANCE §2 step 3 L58:** "**RFC-3161 timestamped** against a trusted timestamp authority." Also flat. BUT this one is immediately followed (L64-81) by a prominent, accurate implementation-status note that explicitly states the engine ships only `LocalHmacAuthority` (on-box HMAC), that RFC-3161 is pluggable, and that HMAC is "tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret, not third-party non-repudiation."

The code confirms: only `LocalHmacAuthority` is a concrete authority; RFC-3161 is a Protocol-only pluggable option; the runnable example and all defaults use HMAC.

This is a genuine docs-vs-code over-claim, strongest in METHODOLOGY (no nearby correction). It's real but MEDIUM-to-LOW: GOVERNANCE self-corrects within the same section, and ADR-0009 + the preregistration module docstring are honest. The defect is the unqualified normative body text presenting third-party non-repudiation (RFC-3161) as what actually happens, when the shipped default provides only on-box tamper-evidence — exactly the property a hostile reviewer keys on.

input.evidence:
CODE: src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py ships exactly one concrete authority class — LocalHmacAuthority (L130). TimestampAuthority (L71) and StampingAuthority (L82) are Protocols only. grep of src/ for class.*Rfc|RFC|Tsa|3161 finds NO concrete RFC-3161 client; RFC-3161 appears only in docstrings/comments as a pluggable option (preregistration.py:22,30,141; discovery.py:89; adjudication.py:137). The runnable example examples/minimal_study.py:36 uses AUTHORITY = LocalHmacAuthority(...), and the lower-level confirm primitives default to a presence predicate (confirm.py:40).

DOCS: docs/METHODOLOGY.md:130-132 states flatly "hypotheses ... are locked and RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation." The following sentence only hedges the adaptive-design aspect (not predictions-before-data); it does NOT qualify the timestamp mechanism, and METHODOLOGY carries no implementation-status note near this line. docs/GOVERNANCE.md:58 step 3 states flatly "RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority."

PARTIAL MITIGATION: GOVERNANCE.md:64-81 immediately follows step 3 with an accurate implementation-status note: "The engine ships one real, data-sovereign authority (LocalHmacAuthority, on-box HMAC) and the Protocol so a study can plug an RFC-3161 TSA. Honest scope: the local authority is tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret, not third-party non-repudiation." ADR-0009 and the preregistration module docstring are likewise honest. So GOVERNANCE self-corrects within the section; METHODOLOGY §7 does not.
input.reasoning:
The finding is factually accurate and not a false positive. The engine ships only LocalHmacAuthority (on-box HMAC); no concrete RFC-3161 TSA client exists in src/ (RFC-3161 is a Protocol-only pluggable option), and all defaults/the runnable example use HMAC. Both body-text claims (METHODOLOGY §7 L131, GOVERNANCE §2 step 3 L58) present "RFC-3161 timestamped" as the mechanism actually in force without an inline qualifier. HMAC provides on-box tamper-evidence, NOT the third-party non-repudiation RFC-3161 implies — a real semantic over-claim on precisely the property a hostile reviewer audits. This is a genuine docs-vs-code drift defect, not style.

However, severity is LOW, not MEDIUM: (1) GOVERNANCE §2's flat claim is immediately corrected by a prominent, accurate implementation-status note in the SAME section (L64-81) that names LocalHmacAuthority, calls RFC-3161 pluggable, and explicitly disclaims third-party non-repudiation — a reader cannot miss it. (2) ADR-0009 and the preregistration docstring are honest. (3) No code behavior is wrong; pre-registration enforcement (content/id binding, verifiable timestamp, ordering) works as documented. The residual real defect is the unqualified METHODOLOGY §7 bullet (which has no nearby corrective note) and the bare GOVERNANCE step-3 line. The suggested fix is correct and minimal: qualify the body text to "cryptographically timestamped (on-box HMAC by default; RFC-3161 TSA pluggable for third-party non-repudiation)." This is a documentation-accuracy fix, not a production code-correctness blocker, hence LOW.

content.text:
Verdict submitted. The finding is REAL but recalibrated to LOW severity.

Summary: The pass-2 assessor is factually correct. The engine ships exactly one concrete authority, `LocalHmacAuthority` (on-box HMAC), at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:130`; RFC-3161 exists only as a Protocol-pluggable option in docstrings, with no concrete TSA client in `src/`. Both body-text lines — `docs/METHODOLOGY.md:130-132` and `docs/GOVERNANCE.md:58` — flatly assert "RFC-3161 timestamped" as the mechanism in force without an inline qualifier, which over-claims third-party non-repudiation when the shipped default delivers only on-box tamper-evidence.

I downgraded MEDIUM to LOW because GOVERNANCE §2's flat claim is immediately followed by an accurate, prominent implementation-status note in the same section (L64-81) that names `LocalHmacAuthority`, marks RFC-3161 pluggable, and explicitly disclaims non-repudiation; ADR-0009 and the module docstring are also honest. The residual genuine defect is the unqualified METHODOLOGY §7 bullet (no nearby corrective note) plus the bare GOVERNANCE step-3 line. No code behavior is wrong — this is a documentation-accuracy fix, and the assessor's suggested wording is the correct remedy.
